title: Run your first program
instruction:
The code is already written.

Just press Run and see what happens.
successMessage: You just made the computer follow an instruction. That is programming.

title: Make it say something different
instruction:
The computer prints whatever you put inside the quotes.

Change "Hello, World!" to your name and run it.
hint: Only change the text between the quotes. Leave everything else exactly as it is.
successMessage: Your program now says something personal. You are in control of what it does.

title: Print two lines
instruction:
One `fmt.Println()` prints one line.

Add a second one below the first and print two things.
hint: Add fmt.Println("something") on a new line inside the curly braces.

codeChecks.message: Add a second fmt.Println() line below the first.

successMessage: The computer ran both lines, top to bottom. That is how all programs work.

title: Print three lines
instruction:
Add one more. Three `fmt.Println()` calls, three lines of output.

Write something different in each one.
hint: You should have three fmt.Println() lines inside func main().
successMessage: Good. You have a program that does three things in order.

title: Where your program starts
instruction:
`func main()` is the entry point of every Go program.

When you press Run, Go finds main and starts executing the code inside the curly braces.

Run this and watch the output.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	fmt.Println("Go starts here")
	fmt.Println("Then runs this")
	fmt.Println("Then this")
}
successMessage: Every Go program you ever write will have a func main(). Now you know why.

title: The import line
instruction:
`import "fmt"` loads a built-in Go package called fmt.

Without it, `fmt.Println` does not exist.

Add two more `fmt.Println()` lines and run it.
hint: Add two new fmt.Println("your text") lines inside the curly braces.

codeChecks.message: Add two more fmt.Println() lines to your program.

successMessage: fmt is a tool. You just used it three times.

title: Spot the mistake
instruction:
Something in this code is wrong.

Run it. Read the error. Fix it.
hint: Look closely at the string inside Println. Count the quote marks.
successMessage: Reading errors and fixing them is most of what programming looks like. You just did it.

title: Another bug to fix
instruction:
This one has a different problem.

Run it, read the error, fix it.
hint: Go functions are case-sensitive. Check the spelling of Println.
successMessage: Go is case-sensitive. fmt.Println works. fmt.println does not. Now you know.

title: Write a program from scratch
instruction:
The main block is empty.

Add three `fmt.Println()` lines and run the program.
hint: Click inside the curly braces and add three fmt.Println("something") lines.

codeChecks.message: Add three fmt.Println() lines inside the curly braces.

successMessage: You wrote a working Go program from an empty file. That is the real thing.

title: Chapter 1 done
instruction:
You know the shape of a Go program.
You can print text.
You can read and fix errors.

Change the message to "Chapter 1 complete" and run it.
hint: Replace the text inside the quotes with: Chapter 1 complete
successMessage: Next up: variables. Your programs are about to get a lot more interesting.
